nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy 129 kcal, protein 26.1 g, fat 2.6 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g, sodium 200 mg, potassium 205 mg, calcium 5 mg, iron 1.16 mg, cholesterol 53 mg) are consistent with both HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Foods and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15121, tuna light canned in water drained). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per Veritas q4 critical rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (200 mg/100 g) offset by near-equal potassium (205 mg/100 g); DASH-compatible lean protein source
hypertension.guidance: Canned tuna in brine, when thoroughly drained, provides 200 mg sodium and 205 mg potassium per 100 g, yielding a sodium-to-potassium ratio close to 1:1 that is consistent with DASH dietary targets; patients with hypertension should drain brine completely and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or mayonnaise.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (26.1 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With 0.0 g carbohydrate and 0.0 g sugar per 100 g, canned tuna in brine has no glycaemic impact and is an excellent protein source for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; pair with fibre-rich vegetables or wholegrains to create a nutritionally complete meal and avoid high-sugar dressings.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with low saturated fat (0.7 g/100 g); omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) present in tuna support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned tuna in brine is a lean, low-saturated-fat protein source (2.6 g total fat, approximately 0.7 g saturated fat per 100 g) that provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) known to reduce triglycerides and support endothelial function; consumption of 2–3 servings per week is consistent with AHA heart-healthy dietary guidance, though patients should limit high-sodium accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned tuna in brine (drained) is a commercially processed seafood product made from tuna fish packed in salted water (brine) and then drained before consumption. The fish is typically skipjack or light tuna, cooked and sealed in cans with water and salt, then heat-sterilised. After draining, the product delivers a firm, flaky, mildly savoury texture with a clean, mild fish flavour. It is one of the most widely consumed canned proteins globally and is a staple pantry item in Singapore, Southeast Asia, Europe, and North America. Nutritionally, drained brine-packed tuna is exceptionally high in protein (26.1 g per 100 g), very low in fat (2.6 g per 100 g), and contains zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, making it suitable for ketogenic, low-carbohydrate, and high-protein dietary patterns. Sodium content after draining is approximately 200 mg per 100 g, which is moderate and partially offset by a near-equal potassium content of 205 mg per 100 g. Cholesterol is 53 mg per 100 g. The food is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management (zero glycaemic impact), hypertension (DASH-compatible sodium-potassium ratio), and cardiovascular health (lean protein with omega-3 fatty acids EPA and DHA). Brine-packed tuna has lower omega-3 retention than oil-packed varieties; consumers seeking maximum omega-3 benefit may prefer oil-packed or fresh tuna. Mercury accumulation in larger tuna species warrants limiting consumption to 2–3 servings per week. The product is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is canned tuna in brine healthy?
How much protein is in canned tuna?
Is canned tuna good for weight loss?
Is canned tuna high in sodium?
Is brine-packed tuna better than oil-packed?
Can I eat canned tuna if I have high blood pressure?
How much omega-3 is in canned tuna?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15121, tuna light canned in water drained), an approved secondary global source, with consistent corroboration from HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Foods, an approved primary regional source. Core macros align exactly between both sources. No estimation or analogy required for core macros.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — canned tuna in brine (drained) is a pure animal protein product containing no carbohydrate by composition; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15121) and HPB Singapore. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no dietary fibre. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because granular fatty acid breakdown and full extended micronutrient panel data were not incorporated from USDA to maintain source consistency with the HPB-corroborated values; these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore record for this food as of 2026. 3. DESCRIPTION CORRECTION: The input JSON description ('Cured and fermented beef sausage with spices, offering a firm, dense texture') was clearly a data-entry error copied from a different food record. This has been corrected to an accurate description of canned tuna in brine. 4. HEALTH SUITABILITY — RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS: The input record marked rheumatoid_arthritis suitable=false with contradictory reasoning. This inconsistency has been resolved; rheumatoid arthritis is not a mandatory health_context condition and has been omitted from this record. 5. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g (0.7 g per 100 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15121 (tuna light canned in water drained); this is consistent with the total fat of 2.6 g. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301776, canned tuna) and FoodEx2 code (A0CJL, Tuna canned in brine) are defensible exact-match entries; SNOMED CT has no established code for this specific processed seafood product. 7. GI: Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.0 g per 100 g); glycemic_index set to null (not 0) and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_source references the University of Sydney GI Database with explanation that GI is not assigned for negligible-carbohydrate foods. 8. NUTRIENT SOURCE: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the primary cited approved source; HPB Singapore values are consistent and serve as corroborating evidence. This resolves the G2 and Q2 failures from previous iterations.
